hook: You already know how to count sequences when every choice must be different — like arranging 3 students from a class of 10, where no student can repeat. But what if repetition is allowed? For example, how many 4-digit PIN codes are possible on a keypad where you can use the same digit more than once? The tools you have right now only handle the no-repetition case, so how do we count when repeats are permitted?
concept: Counting with repetition means that at each position in a sequence, every option is available regardless of what was chosen before. In plain English: you can reuse items. Formally, if you have $n$ distinct options and you are making $r$ independent choices (each with $n$ possibilities), the total number of sequences is $n^r$. This is sometimes called the Multiplication Principle applied uniformly, because the number of choices at every stage is always $n$.
anatomy: The formula $n^r$ has two key components. The base $n$ is the size of the option set — the number of distinct items or values available at each individual choice. The exponent $r$ is the number of positions or selections being made — how many times you draw from that set. The crucial structural feature is independence: because repetition is allowed, each of the $r$ choices is completely independent of the others, so the counts multiply rather than decrease as they would in a permutation without repetition.

worked_example.description_latex: We start by identifying the two components of our formula. A standard PIN uses digits 0 through 9, giving us $n = 10$ options at each position, and the PIN has $r = 4$ positions. Because any digit can repeat, each position is chosen independently from the full set of 10.

We apply the repetition-counting formula directly: multiply $n$ by itself $r$ times, which means raising $n$ to the power $r$. We write $10^4$ because each of the 4 positions independently has 10 choices, and independent choices multiply together.

Evaluating $10^4$ gives us 10,000 possible PIN codes. This confirms our intuition — PINs range from 0000 to 9999, which is exactly 10,000 distinct values — and shows the formula produces a sensible, verifiable answer.

partial_example.description_latex: A combination lock uses 3-letter codes where each letter can be any of the 26 letters in the alphabet, and letters can repeat. We identify $n = 26$ (the alphabet size) and $r = 3$ (the number of positions). Now we need to set up and evaluate the counting formula.
Write the total number of codes using the repetition formula $n^r$. Because each of the 3 positions independently draws from all 26 letters, we raise 26 to the power 3.
Evaluate $26^3$ to find the total number of possible 3-letter codes. Calculate this value and write your answer.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{How many 2-digit sequences can be formed from the digits } \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} \text{ if repetition is allowed?}
\text{A true/false quiz has 6 questions. How many different ways can a student answer the quiz?}
\text{How many 5-character passwords can be made using only lowercase letters (a–z) if repetition is allowed?}

\text{A bag contains 4 colored balls (red, blue, green, yellow). You draw a ball, record its color, and replace it. You do this 3 times. How many distinct ordered records are possible? Compare this count to the number of ordered records if you do NOT replace the ball each time.}
\text{A phone number consists of a 3-digit area code followed by a 7-digit number. Each digit is chosen from 0–9 with repetition allowed. How many distinct phone numbers are possible?}

common_mistakes: Students use $n \times r$ (multiplication without exponentiation) instead of $n^r$. This confuses the formula for independent repeated choices with simple scaling — remember, each additional position multiplies the entire running count by $n$, which is why we need an exponent, not a product.

Students subtract 1 from $n$ for each subsequent position as they would in a no-repetition permutation, writing $n \times (n-1) \times (n-2) \times \cdots$ even when the problem says repetition is allowed. Always check whether items are being replaced or reused before deciding which counting rule to apply.

Students confuse which quantity is $n$ and which is $r$, and compute $r^n$ instead of $n^r$. A helpful check: $n$ is always the size of your option set (what you choose FROM), and $r$ is always the number of selections (how many times you choose), so the base should be the larger option set and the exponent should be the number of positions.
untested_variants: Counting strings or sequences where some positions have different numbers of options (e.g., a password where the first character must be a letter but the remaining characters can be letters or digits).

Using the complement principle together with repetition counting — for example, counting sequences that contain at least one repeated element by subtracting no-repetition permutations from the total with-repetition count.
Repetition problems involving non-uniform sets, such as rolling two different dice with different numbers of faces (e.g., a 6-sided and an 8-sided die) and counting total outcomes.
Counting ordered $r$-tuples drawn from a multiset or a set with restrictions on how many times each specific element can appear (bounded repetition), which goes beyond simple $n^r$.
